Summary of what was found: L’Oréal formally organizes its portfolio into Consumer Products, Luxe, Dermatological Beauty, and Professional Products, supporting premium/luxe and mass/consumer as distinct packaging-evaluation tracks.
Detailed description of what was found: The 2025 annual report lists Consumer Products and Luxe as separate divisions alongside Dermatological Beauty and Professional Products. This supports the report’s structure because one of the world’s largest beauty companies already uses organizational and financial segmentation that mirrors the proposed packaging framework. It supports making Premium/Luxe and Mass-Market/Consumer the primary material-evaluation tracks, while treating dermocosmetics and professional/salon as narrower secondary categories.

Summary of what was found: L’Oréal Luxe defines itself around luxury beauty, elevated services, emotions, and desirability, supporting the view that premium packaging materials serve symbolic and sensory value beyond protection.
Detailed description of what was found: The Luxe Division describes itself as a leader in luxury beauty and emphasizes unique luxury experiences, elevated services, emotions, desirability, and refill as part of redefined beauty rituals. This supports the report’s argument that premium/luxe packs are evaluated partly on emotional, tactile, and experiential performance; therefore, lightweighting in this tier must protect premium cues rather than optimize only for grams or cost.
Source name: L’Oréal Groupe: Luxe Division — L’Oréal Groupe — https://www.loreal.com/en/luxe-division/

Summary of what was found: L’Oréal Consumer Products emphasizes value for money, broad reach, operational scale, and sustainable mass beauty, supporting a distinct mass-market material-selection logic.
Detailed description of what was found: The Consumer Products Division states that it creates beauty for everyone, everywhere, every day, reaches more than one billion consumers, and remains committed to delivering best value for money. It also highlights refill formats such as L’Oréal Paris Elvive refill packs with plastic-saving claims. This supports the mass-market track because it ties the segment to scale, affordability, execution, and material reduction—priorities that typically favor lighter, lower-cost, recyclable, and easier-to-manufacture packaging systems.

Summary of what was found: Coty separates Prestige and Consumer Beauty and explicitly contrasts prestige, mass fragrance, and mass cosmetics across a wide price range.
Detailed description of what was found: Coty’s press release discusses integrating Prestige Beauty and Mass Fragrance while separately reviewing Consumer Beauty, which includes mass cosmetics, mass skin, and personal care. Its financial reporting also separates Prestige and Consumer Beauty revenue pools and notes different pressures, including supply-chain cost issues particularly in Consumer Beauty. This supports the report because it shows that a major beauty player operates with explicit prestige-versus-consumer economics, channel logic, and portfolio expectations that should translate into different lightweight material criteria.
Source name: Coty Announces Plans to Bolster Its Leading Position in Fragrance and Launches a Strategic Review of Its Consumer Beauty Business; Coty Announces Third Quarter Fiscal Year 2026 Results — Coty — https://www.coty.com/news/coty-announces-plans-to-bolster-its-leading-position-in-fragrance-and-launches-a-strategic-review-of-its-consumer-beauty-business ; https://www.coty.com/news/coty-announces-third-quarter-fiscal-year-2026-results

Summary of what was found: Circana tracks prestige and mass beauty as separate retail markets with different scale and pricing profiles, validating brand tier as an industry-standard segmentation.
Detailed description of what was found: Circana reports U.S. prestige beauty and mass beauty as separate retail markets with different sales scale and average-selling-price dynamics. This validates the use of prestige/mass segmentation for packaging-material analysis. It also implies different pack economics: prestige can absorb more material cost and sensory investment, while mass-market operates at larger scale where small unit-level packaging changes can create larger cost and logistics consequences.
Source name: US Prestige & Mass Beauty Retail Performance 2025 — Circana — https://www.circana.com/post/us-prestige-and-mass-beauty-retail-deliver-a-positive-performance-in-2025-circana-reports

Summary of what was found: Circana describes mass beauty as driven by accessible price points and efficacy, while prestige is driven by innovation, elevated formats, and experiential appeal; it also identifies masstige as a bridge segment.
Detailed description of what was found: Circana’s analysis says mass beauty gains momentum as consumers seek high-performing products at accessible price points, while prestige is defined by innovation, elevated formats, and experiential appeal. It also states that masstige skincare brands offer premium benefits at mass prices. This source is useful because it directly frames the consumer logic behind packaging divergence: mass packs need to signal value and performance efficiently, whereas prestige packs rely more on format elevation and experience. It also supports mentioning masstige as a secondary category without displacing the premium-versus-mass core structure.
Source name: U.S. Beauty Market Trends: Consumers Seeking Affordable Alternatives — Circana — https://www.circana.com/post/us-beauty-consumers-are-driven-by-value-based-purchase-behavior

Summary of what was found: McKinsey provides a beauty price-tier ladder including mass, masstige, entry prestige, prestige, and luxury, supporting a two-track report with middle tiers acknowledged.
Detailed description of what was found: McKinsey states that mass beauty is the largest price tier and identifies masstige, entry prestige, prestige, and luxury as higher-price tiers. This is strong evidence for the segmentation logic because it provides an external, recognized tier framework. It supports keeping the report broad enough to mention masstige and entry prestige, but focused enough to consolidate the analysis into two main packaging-material tracks: Premium/Luxe and Mass-Market/Consumer.
Source name: The Beauty Industry Boom: Can Growth Be Maintained? — McKinsey & Company — https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/consumer-packaged-goods/our-insights/the-beauty-boom-and-beyond-can-the-industry-maintain-its-growth

Summary of what was found: McKinsey illustrates how premium packaging can become over-engineered through high-end boxes and nonrecyclable inserts before the consumer reaches the primary pack.
Detailed description of what was found: McKinsey’s packaging article uses a premium-packaging example involving a high-end box and disposable materials such as cellophane or foam before the consumer reaches the bottle. This supports the report’s argument that premium beauty often carries extra secondary-packaging and presentation layers, which can add weight and material complexity for experiential reasons. It explains why premium/luxe lightweighting must preserve unboxing and symbolic cues while reducing unnecessary material burden.
Source name: Packaging: A Key Performance and Value Driver — McKinsey & Company — https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/operations/our-insights/packaging-the-underrated-performance-and-value-driver

Summary of what was found: Shiseido distinguishes Prestige from Premium and also lists Inner Beauty and Life Quality Beauty, supporting secondary category overlays around the premium-versus-mass split.
Detailed description of what was found: Shiseido defines Prestige as high-end, high-added-value cosmetics and fragrances sold mainly through department stores and specialized cosmetic stores, while Premium is defined as mid-priced cosmetics sold mainly in drugstores and general merchandise stores. It also lists Inner Beauty and Life Quality Beauty. This provides a clean source-backed taxonomy for the middle ground between prestige and mass and for adjacent categories that may need to be acknowledged in the report. It supports using prestige/luxe and consumer/mass as the primary tracks while treating premium/masstige-like and special-needs categories as secondary variants.

Summary of what was found: L’Oréal Dermatological Beauty provides a source-backed example of dermocosmetics as a distinct but secondary category focused on skin health, efficacy, and professional trust.
Detailed description of what was found: L’Oréal says Dermatological Beauty sits at the crossroads of beauty and health, is a dermocosmetic market leader, and serves needs ranging from skin pathologies to aesthetics. It also mentions accessible refill solutions. This supports the report because dermocosmetics can have different formulation-protection and trust cues than mainstream beauty, but they do not need to displace the two main tracks. They can be treated as an overlay focused on efficacy, safety, and clinical credibility.

Summary of what was found: P&G states that packaging must protect products, delight consumers, reduce plastic, create transportation efficiencies, and scale circularity through recyclable or reusable formats.
Detailed description of what was found: P&G’s ESG waste page states that superior packaging protects, ships, and supports product use, while new packaging solutions should delight consumers, reduce plastic, create transportation efficiencies, and reuse materials. It also describes progress through recycled plastic, lightweight designs, replacement of plastic with renewable materials, and recyclable/reusable packaging. This is directly relevant to the mass-market packaging track because it connects material selection to manufacturability, logistics, at-scale recyclability, and system feasibility—not only aesthetics.
Source name: ESG — Environmental — Waste — Procter & Gamble Investor Relations — https://www.pginvestor.com/esg/environmental/waste/default.aspx

Summary of what was found: Unilever’s plastics strategy emphasizes lighter packaging, recycled content, rigid-versus-flexible recyclability realities, reusable/refill models, and alternatives that work in practice and at scale.
Detailed description of what was found: Unilever reports progress against reusable, recyclable, or compostable packaging goals and distinguishes rigid and flexible plastic recyclability realities. It says it is reducing virgin plastic by using recycled plastic, designing lighter packaging, and scaling alternative materials and models that work in practice and at scale. This strongly supports the mass/consumer packaging track because it shows how a scaled consumer-goods company evaluates materials through operational recyclability, infrastructure compatibility, and deployment economics, often ahead of prestige-style sensory cues.
Source name: How Unilever Is Tackling Plastic Waste — Unilever — https://www.unilever.com/sustainability/plastics/

Summary of what was found: Aptar links mono-material dispensing systems with recyclability, showing why mass and consumer packs often favor material simplification.
Detailed description of what was found: Aptar’s Future pump is described as a polyethylene-only dispensing system developed so that the pump aligns with common bottle material streams. Aptar states that traditional pumps can contain multiple materials, including metal, which complicates recycling, while mono-material design makes the complete packaging easier to recycle. This supports the report’s material-selection implication that efficiency-led packaging programs should simplify component materiality, align closures with bottle streams, and reduce separation burdens. This is highly relevant for mass-market packs and for lightweighting pathways that must preserve high throughput and recycling compatibility.
Source name: Aptar Beauty Introduces Future: a Fully Recyclable Mono-Material Pump — Aptar — https://aptar.com/en-us/news-events/aptar-introduces-a-fully-recyclable-mono-material-pump

Summary of what was found: Bormioli Luigi shows that premium beauty still values decoration, refillability, and glass aesthetics, while combining those demands with lighter walls, improved weight-to-strength ratio, and PCR content.
Detailed description of what was found: Bormioli Luigi says leading beauty brands are embracing refillable bottles whose decorative elements underscore value and extended lifespan. It also says it is optimizing weight-to-strength ratio and glass composition, offering lighter, thinner-walled premium solutions while preserving design rituals and customization. This demonstrates that premium/luxe lightweighting is real, but differs from mass lightweighting: the solution must preserve prestige codes, decoration potential, formula compatibility, and long-life or refill value.
Source name: Bormioli Luigi Invests in Innovative Eco-Conscious Packaging — Bormioli Luigi — https://bormioliluigi.com/beauty/en/news/104/bormioli-luigi-invests-in-innovative-eco-conscious-packaging

Summary of what was found: Quadpack links tactile finishes, emotional connection, craftsmanship, luxury codes, and eco-design, supporting the role of material feel and storytelling in premium packaging.
Detailed description of what was found: Quadpack’s BeautyWood 2026 release says wooden beauty packaging focuses on tactile finishes that create emotional connection, with forms aligned to luxury codes and eco-design principles. It also notes sustainably managed wood and waste-minimizing manufacturing methods. This source explains why premium packaging may use materials such as wood, glass-like plastics, decorative components, or metal accents: they communicate tactility, craftsmanship, and narrative value. It also shows how premium lightweighting may rely on premium-feel, lower-impact substitutions rather than straightforward downgauging.
Source name: Touch Flow Feel – the Sensory Appeal of Wood for Beauty — Quadpack — https://www.quadpack.com/assets/14383132/touch-flow-feel-the-sensory-appeal-of-wood-for-beauty/
